title: Abstract
content: h i g h l i g h t sMMTTiO 2 catalyst coated over monolith was tested for CO 2 photoreduction with H 2 O. Microchannel monolith showed high CO 2 conversion to fuels compared to cell reactor.Highest yield rates obtained were 139 and 52 lmole g catal.A1 h A1 for CH 4 and CO.L-H model showed competitive adsorption with efficient CO 2 adsorption on MMTTiO 2 .Kinetic model revealed photooxidation-reduction process over MMTTiO 2 catalyst.

title: Experimental
content: Fig. 1 describes the procedure for catalyst synthesis and coating on monolith channels.The sol-gel single step method was used to prepare TiO 2 nanoparticles and MMT modified TiO 2 nanocomposites.The precursory of titanium solution was prepared with molar ratios: Ti(C 4 H 9 O) 4 :15C 2 H 5 OH:2CH 3 COOH (1 M).Typically, 30 ml of isopropanol was added in 10 ml of titanium tetra iso-propoxide and stirred well for 30 min.The slow hydrolysis process was conducted by adding mixtures of 6.37 ml acetic acid (1 M) and 10 ml isopropanol.The system was vigorously stirred for 24 h at 30 C.Subsequently, MMT dissolved in isopropanol was added drop wise and stirred for another 6 h until clear sol was formed.The prepared sol was aged for additional 6 h to get thick gel type sol.The sol obtained was poured into a glass container for monolith coating while the remaining sol was used for MMTTiO 2 nanocomposite synthesis. The monolith was initially washed with acetone to remove any organic material, and then dried at 80 C for 12 h.After the weight of the dried monolith was recorded, it was immersed slowly into the MMT loaded TiO 2 sol and kept for a few minutes.The excess sol from the channels was blown off using compressed air and dried with an air drier.To increase the film thickness and catalyst loading, the monolith was dipped for the second time using the same procedure.The coated monolith was then put into the oven, dried at 80 C for 24 h and finally calcined in a muffle furnace at a rate of 5 C min A1 up to a maximum of 500 C and held for 5 h.MMTTiO 2 sol was dried and calcined with the same procedure as explained above to obtain MMTTiO 2 powder.For comparison, bare TiO 2 nanoparticles were also similarly prepared and coated inside the microchannels of the monolith. In order to determine the structure and crystalinity of the photocatalysts, powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on Bruker D8 advance diffractometer (Cu Ka radiation, wavelength k 1.54 A, operated at 40 kV and 40 mA).The surface morphology was examined using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE SEM) with Carl Zeiss Supra 35 VP FE-SEM instrument.The crystallite shape and dispersion of TiO 2 at MMT galleries were observed using TEM and HR-TEM with FEI-Tecni G2 Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) at EFGO Scientific located at Kulim Hi Tech, Kedah, Malaysia.Textural characterization of the samples was carried out with a Micromeritics ASAP 2020.The nitrogen adsorption-desorption properties were examined at 77 K.The specific surface area (S BET ) of monolayer coverage was determined using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method.The pore size distribution was obtained from the adsorption branch of the isotherm by means of Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method.UV-Vis absorption spectra of the samples were measured with Cary 300 Agilent spectrophotometer. The photocatalytic activity for reduction of CO 2 with H 2 O in gaseous phase was evaluated in a microchannel monolith based photoreactor and its performance was compared with a cell type photoreactor.The schematic of the monolith photoreactor is shown in Fig. 2a.The reactor consisted of stainless steel cylindrical vessel of length 5.5 cm and total volume 150 cm 3 .Heating and cooling jackets were applied to adjust the reactor temperature.The monoliths used in the present study were supplied by Pingxiang Meitao Chemical Packing Co., Ltd., China.Each monolith diameter was 6 cm, length 5 cm and channels per square inch (CPSI) of 100 and 400.The nanocatalysts (about 50 mg) were coated uniformly as a thin film inside the microchannels of the monolith.The surface coated monolith was inserted inside the cylindrical reactor.Photoreactor has a quartz window to allow light to irradi-ate from a 200 W mercury reflector lamp for UV irradiation source.The heat from the lamp was removed using a cooling fan attached at the top and on the sides of the lamp.The lamp was enclosed with a cover and aluminum foil to exclude any light from an external source, prevent UV leakage and maximum light intensity within the reactor.The light intensity was measured with an optical process monitor, ILT OPM-1D, and SED008W sensor that was placed inside the reactor prior to the experiment.In case of cell type photoreactor, the reactor chamber was the same as that of the monolith reactor (L 5.5 cm, V 150 cm 3 ) as illustrated in Fig. 2b.For the photocatalytic activity test, the same source of light was repeated used as that of the monolith reactor.However, 50 mg of nanocatalyst powder was suspended uniformly at the bottom of the reactor to ensure efficient light distribution through the catalyst surface. Both the reactors were purged using helium (He) flow and checked for leakage at 2 bar pressure for several hours prior to gas feeding.Compressed CO 2 (99.999) regulated by a mass flow controller (MFC) was bubbled through water saturator to carry moisture.The concentration of CO 2 was controlled using helium carrier gas (99.999).The reactors were purged for an hour using a mixture of CO 2 and helium prior to the start of the experiment.The pressure of CO 2 and He (helium) gas mixtures inside the reactor was maintained at 0.40 bars above atmospheric pressure. The gaseous products from the reactors were taken using a gastight syringe (Agilent, 1000 ll) at regular intervals.The products were analyzed with a gas chromatograph (GC-Agilent Technologies 6890N, USA) equipped with FID and TCD detectors (GCFIDTCD).The FID detector was connected with HP PLOT Q column while the TCD detector consisted of Porapak Q, DC-200 and MS columns.

title: Results and discussion
content: Fig. 3 presents the comparative XRD patterns of all the samples.The peaks of TiO 2 revealed a pure crystalline and anatase phase calcined at 500 C.The addition of MMT into TiO 2 also produced pure anatase phase of TiO 2 .However, the MMT containing TiO 2 nanocomposites have dissimilar XRD patterns than the pure MMT, which have no MMT peaks.This indicates that the layered structures of MMT have been destroyed.However, the diffraction peak around 2h 25.50(10 1) of MMT modified TiO 2 nanocomposites became weaker and wider.Similar trends could be seen for all TiO 2 anatase peaks.These results confirmed that the layered structure of silicate in MMT has an effect on MMT crystal lattice containing nanocomposites.This also reveals that the addition of MMT in the system allowed the silicate layer of MMT to behave as a barrier, which prevented the agglomeration and natural crystallization of TiO 2 sol.The average crystallite size of the samples was calculated using Scherer's equation according to the (1 0 1) peak of TiO 2 34,35. where L is the thickness of crystallite (nm), k is a constant depending on crystallite shape (0.90 for this study), k is the X-ray wavelength (nm), a is the full peak width at half max in radians and h is Bragg's angle of the 2h peak.The crystallite sizes of TiO 2 , and MMTTiO 2 nanocomposites were 22 nm and 17.85 nm respectively.The size of TiO 2 decreased gradually with MMT loading.SEM micrographs of bare monolith and catalyst coated layers on monolith channels are reported in Fig. 4a-d.Fig. 4a exhibits the pore morphology of the bare monolith.All the pores were entirely filled with a thin layer of catalyst coating inside the monolith channels as shown in Fig. 4b andc.Fig. 4d indicates the uniform layer of catalyst investigated in 10-lm magnification of the SEM.The catalyst layer was almost smooth and thoroughly distributed over the surface of the monolith.The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) of 10 wt. MMTTiO 2 nanocomposite are depicted in Fig. 5a andb TiO 2 nanoparticles into the interlayers of MMT destroyed the ordered structure of MMT, resulting in delaminated TiO 2 -pillared montmorillonite.TiO 2 nanoparticles were formed into the interlayers and on the surface of MMT during hydrolysis process.Fig. 5b shows the lattice fringe spacing of TiO 2 nanoparticle inside MMT galleries having value about to 0.35 nm that clearly relates to anatase phase of TiO 2 .Thus, TEM results confirmed efficient distribution of anatase TiO 2 nanoparticles over the silicates layers at MMT galleries. The adsorption isotherm, pore size distribution, and cumulative surface area of catalyst samples are shown in Fig. 6.Fig. 6a exhibit the N 2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of pure TiO 2 and MMTTiO 2 , respectively.The isotherms present typical type IV IUPAC curve with hysteresis loops, characteristics of mesoporous materials and associated with capillary condensation 36.The well-defined hysteresis loops with a steep desorption branches and less steep adsorption branches for all materials belong to H1-type, indicating effective radii of mesoporous bodies with orders cylindrical pore geometry and a high degree of pore size uniformity 37. The initial part of the isotherms (at the low PP o ) are attributed to monolayer-multilayer adsorption on the internal surface of the materials.The steep increment at the higher PP o was due to capillary condensation within pores followed by saturation as the pores were filled with liquid.The binding energy between adsorbate and surface for the first layer (known as monolayer) is called esoteric heat of adsorption.Binding adsorption energy for all layers subsequent to monolayers is referred to as molar heat of condensation.The monolayer-multilayer was more dominant in TiO 2 in which capillary action started at PP o 0.55.However, capillary and condensation action was more prominent in MMTiO 2 composite and was eminent at PP o 0.42.Fig. 6b portrays the BJH pore size distribution of mesoporous TiO 2 and MMTTiO 2 nanoparticles.The pore sizes distribution curves of all the samples were determined from a BJH adsorption branch of the isotherm, which displays single distribution peak.The TiO 2 pore size distribution peak was in the range of 4-26 nm while the peaks of MMT modified TiO 2 exists in the range of 2.6-14.6 nm. The surface area, pore volume and pore size of all samples are summarized in Table 1.The BET surface area was 42.98 m 2 g A1 and 51.79 m 2 g A1 for bare TiO 2 nanoparticles and 10 wt. MMT loading, respectively.Similar trends were observed in external surface area, yet BJH total pore volumes were decreased slightly with addition of MMT.However, the pore size of TiO 2 decreased from 10.33 nm to 8.56 nm with MMT loading due to the strong influence of MMT on crystal growth, resulting in smaller particle size. The UV-Vis absorbance spectra of TiO 2 nanoparticles and MMT modified TiO 2 nanocomposites are depicted in Fig. 7.The absorption band edge of TiO 2 appeared around 402 nm, which has red shift compared with intrinsic anatase TiO 2 (energy gap is 3.20 eV).The absorption band edge of MMT modified TiO 2 samples were located at 398 and 396 nm for 10 MMTTiO 2 and 20 MMT TiO 2 , respectively.It is obvious that MMT has no effect on shifting band edge.The light absorption of purified MMT increased to become almost transparent in the wavelength longer than 250 nm.Similar trends for light absorption are reported in literature over MMT K10 38.The band gap energy was calculated according to where 'E g ' is band gap energy (J), 'h' is Planks constant (6.626A 10 A34 J s), 'C' is light speed (3.0 A 10 8 ms) and 'k' is cutoff wavelength (m).The cut-off wavelength was calculated using linear extrapolation from the infection point of the curve to the base line.The E bg estimated were 3.09, 3.12 and 3.13 eV for TiO 2 , 10 MMTTiO 2 and 20 MMTTiO 2 respectively.The band gaps increased slightly with MMT suggesting MMT has no effect in shifting band gap toward visible region. Firstly, the prepared catalyst samples were investigated for preliminary testing under UV irradiations under the following conditions: (1) monolith without catalyst and helium; (2) monolith and TiO 2 catalyst with helium; and (3) monolith coated with MMTTiO 2 photocatalyst and helium.In all sets, no reaction products were detected even after 2 h of the irradiations time at 373 K.In addition, additional preliminary tests using H 2 O vapors and helium in the presence of photocatalyst were conducted and again no carbon-containing compounds were detected.Therefore, all the carbon-based products reported in this study were produced from CO 2 through photocatalytic reactions. The effect of cell density on photocatalytic CO 2 reduction over different irradiation time is illustrated in Fig. 8a.The results indicated that the higher CH 4 yield rate was produced over TiO 2 using monolith with 100 CPSI cell density.The higher yield rate was due to efficient utilization of light irradiations and due to the higher illuminated surface area of catalyst coated on the inner side of the microchannels.However, CH 4 yield rate was lower when the monolith cell density of 400 CPSI was employed.As the CPSI and cell density increased, aperture size was considerably smaller, resulting in inefficient irradiation of lights to pass through the channels.Under such circumstances, only the front part of the channels would be fully illuminated while the inner channel received only a small portion of the total irradiation, which ultimately reduced the reaction rate.Although higher cell density for monolith reactor usually improves conversion rates, but in photocatalytic reactors an optimum cell density is critical for efficient light distribution to maximize yield rates. Fig. 8b shows the effect of monolith length on photocatalytic CO 2 reduction over TiO 2 photocatalyst at different irradiation times.It was found that CO 2 photoreduction with H 2 O vapors to CH 4 was increased by increasing the length of monolith under UV light irradiations.However, by increasing the channel length to 5 cm, a gradual reduction in yield of CH 4 was observed.The decreased in yield with longer channels can be attributed to inefficient distribution of light irradiation.As light intensity decreased along the axial length and beyond a critical reduced distance, monolith channels received very little light.Therefore, an optimum monolith length is critical to enhance the conversion of CO 2 and yield rates.Basically, in photocatalytic reactor design, the challenging task, which always remained a priority, is effective utilization of radiant energy for maximum reaction rate.Photon flux distribution inside the reactor cannot be disregarded, since it directly influences reactor performance and is helpful for interpretation of kinetic data 39.It has also been observed by other researchers that only the first part of the reactor could be fully irradiated and the intensity of the incident light decreased rapidly along the channel length 40. Fig. 9 illustrates the effect of reaction time on yield of different products using 10 MMT modified TiO 2 nanoparticles coated over monolith microchannels.The yield of all the products increased efficiently until the 10th hour and beyond this, there was a gradual increase in the yield until it reached to steady state.CH 4 and CO were found to be the major products with adequate amount of C 2 H 4 , C 2 H 6 , C 3 H 6 and C 3 H 8 hydrocarbons in the product mixture.As per anticipation, C 2 -C 3 paraffins and olefins compounds were produced over MMTTiO 2 supported monolith.The yields of all the products were in the order of (lmole g catal.A1 ) CH 4 CO C 3- H 8 C 2 H 6 C 2 H 4 C 3 H 6 .The observed selectivity corresponded to 69.0 and 25.8 for CH 4 and CO, respectively.The significant amount of CH 4 along with other higher hydrocarbons (C 2 -C 3 paraffins and olefins) reveals efficient production of electrons and their utilizations during photocatalytic CO 2 reduction process.This phenomenon could be due to the higher illuminated surface area of catalysts per unit volume, higher photonic efficiency and efficient mass transfer inside the microchannels of monolith.This was also supposedly because of efficient adsorption of the reactants and desorption of products over the very thin film of catalyst 24.Furthermore, a significant amount of hydrocarbons confirmed the multistep chain reactions over the MMTTiO 2 coated microchannels monolith due to significant production of electron-hole pairs.On the other hand, highly dispersed MMT into TiO 2 structure served as an electron trap and hindered recombination of photogenerated charges.It is probable that the addition of MMT might have an effect on the reaction path during CO 2 reduction.In other words, MMT is a promising material to enhance TiO 2 photoreduction efficiency and is more favorable for the production of higher hydrocarbons. Fig. 10 compares the performance of the monolith and the cell photoreactor.TiO 2 dispersed inside the cell type reactor shows negligible photoactivity compared to TiO 2 coated inside microchannels.The yield of CH 4 was increased from 7.7 to 82 lmole g catal.A1 by replacing cell type reactor with microchannel monolith photoreactor.The production of CH 4 further increased using monolith reactor and MMTTiO 2 catalyst.The CH 4 yield in monolith photoreactor was 78 higher than cell type reactor over MMTTiO 2 catalyst.Similar trends could be seen in the production of CO using both types of reactor systems.This phenomenon can be explained based on the illuminated surface area available for CO 2 reduction.In catalyst suspended over the bottom of the reactor, the exposed surface area available for absorption of light irradiations is much lower as compared to catalyst coated over microchannels.The lower yield rate in the cell type reactor was also due to low light utilization efficiency.There are also mass transfer limitations in the cell type reactors 24,33.Conversely, higher conversion efficiency has been demonstrated with the monolith photoreactor due to its larger surface area and more efficient light utilization resulting in higher photonic efficiency.The highest efficiency in monolith photoreactor is supposedly because of the higher mass transfer rate and CO 2 adsorption inside the microchannels due to very thin film coating having negligible diffusional effects 33,41. Table 2 highlights the optimal parameters used in both the systems for photocatalytic CO 2 with H 2 O.Under the same experimental conditions and using monolith photoreactor, CH 4 yield over MMT modified TiO 2 was 139 lmole g catal.A1 h A1 , which was 18.5fold higher compared to cell reactor.The yield rates of all reaction products using both types of photoreactor systems and catalysts are summarized in Table 3.The performance of the photoreactor for CH 4 production rate was in the order of MMT-TiO 2 -monolith (139 lmole g catal.A1 h A1 ) TiO 2 -monolith (82 lmole g catal.A1h A1 ) MMT-TiO 2 -SS cell (43 lmole g catal.A1 h A1 ). TiO 2 -SS cell (7.7 lmole g catal.A1 h A1 ).Similarly, the yield rates of CO produced were 52, 47, 25 and 5.2 lmole g catal.A1 h A1 for MMT-TiO 2 -monolith, TiO 2 -monolith, MMT-TiO 2 -cell and TiO 2 -cell, respectively.A large number of higher hydrocarbons such as C 2 H 4 , C 2 H 6 , C 3 H 6 and C 3 H 8 were formed when MMT modified TiO 2 catalyst supported monolith was used.These products were detected because of the large number of electrons generated over MMTTiO 2 supported microchannels.This was also because MMT served as electron traps to inhibit the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs.On the other hand, the surface hydroxyl (OH) groups over a MMT TiO 2 catalyst as a thin layer over microchannels increased CO 2 adsorption, which enhanced the photocatalytic activity.In general, higher photoactivity of TiO 2 in the presence of MMT and monolith may be attributed to the followings: (1) Larger illuminated surface area, higher photonic efficiency due to efficient light absorption and utilization, and minimum diffusion effects could improve yield rates in monolith photoreactor.Conversely, in cell type photoreactor, there is low light utilization efficiency and lower mass transfer rates which results in lower yield rates. (2) TiO 2 nanoparticles coated over microchannels gave higher yield due to larger illuminated surface area as compared to the cell type reactor.The smaller particles also hindered recombination of electron-hole pairs.(3) In MMTTiO 2 nanocomposites, layered structure of silicate in MMT controls the crystal growth, resulting in smaller particle size and higher surface area.MMTTiO 2 nanocomposites with smaller particle sizes coated over microchannels have higher photocatalytic activity, as more electrons are more mobile on the delaminated TiO 2 montmorillonite.In the galleries of MMT, there are possible d-orbits vacant cations that could trap electrons during TiO 2 photocatalysis, and possibly enhanced photocatalytic activity. In photocatalytic CO 2 reduction with H 2 O vapors, the first step is the production of electron-hole pairs on the catalyst surface under light irradiations.In the second step, produced electrons (eA) are transferred from the conduction band of TiO 2 toward CO 2 yielding A CO A 2 radicals.Holes (h ) are transferred toward H 2 O for initiating photooxidation and as a result hydrogen ions (H ) and hydroxyl radical ( A OH) are produced.The H 2 O is further oxidized with A OH radicals and produced O 2 and H 42.The A H radicals produced during the reduction of proton were reacted with carbon radicals on the catalyst surface to produce intermedi- a The dimension of monolith used for comparing the performance were length 2.5 cm, CPSI 100, diameter 6 cm.b Yield rates were calculated at 10 h irradiation basis, P H2 O 0.042 bar and P CO2 of 0.040 bar. Since all the above products were detected experimentally, it could be assumed that a large number of electron-hole pairs were produced over MMT modified TiO 2 catalyst coated over microchannels, yielding intermediate product CO, and finally C 1 to C 3 hydrocarbons.Similar observations were reported by Saladin and Alxneit 43 during gas phase reduction of CO 2 with H 2 O over TiO 2 surface.The reaction scheme for the production of CH 4 and higher hydrocarbons using MMTTiO 2 supported microchannel is elaborated further in Fig. 11. The photocatalytic CO 2 transformation to CO and CH 4 over MMT modified TiO 2 could be further explained using the energy band theory, which is based on the relative positions of conductance band, valance band and oxidation potentials.In general, photo-excited electrons could consume effectively, if the reduction potential of reaction is lower than the conductance band potential of the semiconductor 44.The possible reactions that can occur during photocatalytic CO 2 with H 2 O to produce CO and CH 4 in terms of thermodynamic reduction potentials versus normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) at pH 7 can be described by reactions ( 8)-( 11) 8,45,46. The conductance band potential of TiO 2 is E CB A0.50V at pH 7 which is more feasible to derive CO 2 reduction to CH 4 because of the lower reduction potential difference (E o (CO 2 CH 4 ) A0.24V).A significant production of CO was due to the reduction potential, E o (CO 2 CO) A0.48V which is also suitable for its production.Although the reduction potential in Eq. ( 10) is much lower than Eq. ( 8), it requires 8 electrons to produce CH 4 compared to 2 electrons for CO.However, the high CH 4 yield rate over MMTTiO 2 catalyst coated in microchannels led to an increased probability of multi-electron reactions (eight electrons for CH 4 production) due to their significant generation and mobility inside microchannels over the catalyst surface. In heterogeneous catalysis, the kinetic expression could be developed by applying the principals of mass action in terms of surface concentrations.The surface reaction rates are supposed to depend on the fraction of sites covered by different species.The rates are usually assumed to be proportional to the concentrations of reactants raised to a simple power.For an irreversible bimolecular reaction between the molecules that are competitively adsorbed on the same type of active sites, the reaction rate depends on the probability that molecules are on adjacent sites.Furthermore, this probability may also be proportional to the product of fractional coverage.The bimolecular photocatalytic reaction for CO 2 reduction can be explained by using There are five useful steps in the photocatalytic reaction mechanism. Step one is the adsorption of reactant to active sites; step two consists of light adsorption and production of electrons and holes on the catalyst surface.Step three is the interaction of charge particle with adsorbed species and recombination of charge particles. Step four consists of oxidation and reduction reactions while the fifth step is desorption of product from the catalyst surface. The surface reactions are usually assumed to be the slow step and eventually the rate controlling step.If the reactants are competitively adsorbed on the same adsorption sites with different rate constants, then such reactions can be postulated by a Langmuir-Hinshelwood (L-H) mechanism.For photocatalytic type reactions, when CO 2 and H 2 O were assumed to be adsorbed on the same active sites on the catalyst surface, the rate of Eq. ( 12) could be obtained by using L-H model as illustrated in where k is the rate constant of any particular product, I is UV flux intensity in which kinetic constants are evaluated and is different than local UV flux.In general, the photocatalytic reaction rate is proportional to I a , where a is the reaction order of light intensity, having value one or less depending light intensity.K H 2 O ; K CO 2 ; K CO ; K O 2 and K CH 4 are the ratios of rate constants for adsorption and desorption of H 2 O, CO 2 , CO, O 2 and CH 4 , respectively.Eq. ( 13) can be simplified further by assuming only reactants are adsorbed on the catalyst surface while all products desorbed immediately after chemical reaction.Thus, CO 2 and H 2 O were adsorbed on surface of the catalyst and the modified form of Eq. ( 13) becomes The constants of the L-H model were determined by correlating it with experimental data.It has been observed that the reaction rate is proportional to P CO 2 at a lower partial pressure when only a small fraction of the sites are occupied.However, as P CO 2 increase, the rate becomes less dependent on it, as illustrated in the L-H model profile (Fig. 12).The best fitted rate constants k, and adsorption equilibrium constants K H 2 O and K CO 2 are listed in Table 4 The kinetic model for photocatalytic CO 2 reduction with H 2 O to formulate a reaction product could be developed using experimental results, as discussed previously.Some of the products would photo-adsorb on the catalyst surface, blocked the active sites and slowed down the CO 2 reduction process.There is also the possibility some of these products recombined in a reverse reaction.When CO and O 2 were not desorbing effectively from the catalyst surface, these products would undergo photo-oxidation back into CO 2 in the reversed reaction.The kinetic model can be developed to investigate the coupled effect of adsorptive photocatalytic reduction and oxidation processes.It is obvious from a L-H model that water adsorption constant K H 2 O value was less than one, indicating very weak adsorption, while CO 2 was adsorbed very strongly over MMT modified TiO 2 surface.Assuming, that CO 2 covered most of the surface area, L-H model can be simplified (Eq.( 14)) A simple kinetic model incorporating the coupled effect of the adsorptive photocatalytic reduction and oxidation could be developed using the modified Langmuir-Hinshelwood model (Eq.( 15)), using the following assumptions: (1) The immediate reduction of CO 2 on the surface produced CO, which strongly adsorb and overall rate of reaction may be limited by the rate of CO desorption; (2) Initially the reaction rate was very fast but the rate decreases as CO accumulated on the surface, strongly adsorbed and the surface nearly completely covered by CO i.e. where k 1 (k red I a ) is the reduction rate constant dependent on temperature and photon intensity.During photocatalytic CO 2 reduction, when CO entirely covers the catalyst surface, then it also undergoes partial oxidation with O 2 .The Langmuir-Hinshelwood model could also be used to evaluate the rate of photo-oxidation.As discussed previously, there was also a significant production of CO in products, which reveals it's efficient desorption, so the rate of oxidation will be much slower as compared to a reduction.When CO oxidation reaction with O 2 undergoes the dissociative adsorption process, the L-H mechanism could be written as explained in Eq. ( 17) 32.The rate of product formation can be explained by subtracting the rate of oxidation reaction from rate of reduction as explained in where k 2 (k ox I a ) is the oxidation rate constant and is dependent on temperature and light intensity factor.Under constant temperature and pressure, partial pressure of CO 2 and water vapors will be constant.Similarly, partial pressures of CO and O 2 would be proportional to the partial pressure of desired product i.e.P CO 14 P O 2 14 P. Based on these assumptions, the net reduction equation (Eq.( 18)) could be simplified as illustrated by where The differential equation (Eq.( 20)) was solved using an integral approach as given by Eq. ( 21).Applying initial boundary condition (t ?t o , P o 0), constant C could be calculated.Where, t o is the reaction startup time or time delay of photo-activities due to transient state at which P o 0.After inserting the value of C and simplifying, Eqs. ( 22)-( 24) are obtained. where Eq. ( 24) is the desired kinetic equation and it could be used to evaluate the experimental data.However, it is further simplified by applying k 7 ) k 8 P 12 k 9 P as the values of k 8 P 12 and k 9 p values will be much smaller than k 7 , the simplified form of Eq. ( 24) could be obtained as explained in Eq. ( 25) is the simplified kinetic model equation while k 5 , k 6 and k 7 are the constants related to reaction rate constants, adsorptiondesorption ratio constants, light intensity, and experimental conditions.By assuming ideal gas law, P can be measured as the yield of desired product at any interval of time t.This kinetic model would be suitable for most of the photocatalytic CO 2 reduction processes in gaseous phase in which there will be a strong movement of electrons at the catalyst surface and efficient conversion of CO to other products or desorption of CO from the catalyst surface.The kinetic equation should be useful for optimization purposes and for deducing the rate and product yield at any particular reaction time. The curves representing the profiles of CH 4 production over TiO 2 and MMT modified TiO 2 as a function of irradiation time using the proposed kinetic model (Eq.( 25)) is shown in Fig. 13.After iterating the constants in the proposed expression, the model has fitted well to the experimental data using constants as summarized in Table 5.The best fitting of the model with experimental data confirmed photocatalytic reduction and oxidation process over the catalyst while CO was strongly adsorbed compared to other products.It may also be attributed to the possibility of strong electrons mobility, and efficient desorption of products over the coated monolith surface.Furthermore, in the photocatalytic reduction process, these products could hinder the mass transfer of intermediates over the catalyst surface if not effectively desorbed.When product adsorption on the catalyst surface remained longer, there will be higher chances of collisions among the products, resulting in reverse photo-oxidation reaction.However, this probability

title: Conclusions
content: Photocatalytic CO 2 reduction with water vapors over MMT modified TiO 2 catalyst in microchannel monolith photoreactor was conducted in this study.The experimental results present significant new findings to improve CO 2 conversion efficiency.Modification of TiO 2 with MMT produced more controlled crystal growth and anatase phase of TiO 2 .Loading MMT into TiO 2 structure increased surface area with smaller particle size.The experimental results revealed efficient CO 2 photoreduction with H 2 O to CO and C 1 -C 3 hydrocarbons over MMTTiO 2 supported microchannel monolith compared to bare TiO 2 and cell type reactor.The maximum yield rate of CH 4 and CO were 139 and 52 lmole g catal.A1 h A1 , respectively at reaction temperature 373 K, P CO 2 14 0:040 bar and P H 2 O 0:042 bar.The significant increase in the yield rate indicated photon energy was effectively utilized in monolith reactor compared with cell type reactor.The Langmuir-Hinshelwood model revealed that yield rates of products are dependent on efficient adsorption of the reactants and desorption of products over the catalyst surface.The kinetic model confirmed photocatalytic adsorptive reduction and oxidation reaction over the catalyst surface.It also revealed higher mobility of electron on the catalyst surface and efficient desorption of products, resulting in higher product yield rates.Therefore, it can be concluded that for CO 2 reduction applications microchannel monolith is an efficient reactor while MMTTiO 2 an effective photocatalyst for maximizing yield rates. Fig. 1. Preparation of MMTTiO 2 nanocomposites and MMT loaded TiO 2 washcoat on monolith channels. Fig. 2. Schematic of experimental setup for photocatalytic CO 2 reduction with H 2 O vapors: (a) monolith photoreactor and (b) cell type photoreactor. Fig. 4. SEM micrographs of bare monolith and MMTTiO 2 coated monolith at different magnifications: (a) bare monolith, (b) top sectional view of catalyst coated channel at magnification 500 lm, (c) cross sectional view of catalyst coated channel at magnification 500 lm, and (d) SEM image at magnification 10 lm. Fig. 6.Adsorption isotherm and pore size distribution TiO 2 and MMTTiO 2 nanocomposite: (a) N 2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of TiO 2 and MMTTiO 2 samples; (b) BJH pore size distribution of corresponding samples. Fig. 7. UV-Vis absorption spectra of TiO 2 , MMT and MMT modified TiO 2 nanocomposites. Fig. 9. Photocatalytic CO 2 reduction with H 2 O to CO and C 1 -C 3 hydrocarbons over MMTTiO 2 coated monolith at PH 2 O 0.042 bar and PCO 2 of 0.040 bar (CPSI 100, L 2.5 cm, dia 6 cm). Fig. 10.Comparison between cell type and monolith photoreactor for photocatalytic CO 2 reduction over TiO 2 and MMTTiO 2 catalysts at PH 2 O 0.042 bar and PCO 2 of 0.040 bar) (CPSI 100, L 2.5 cm, dia 6 cm). Fig. 11.(a) Schematic of reaction scheme for reduction of CO 2 with H 2 O to various products and (b) adsorption-desorption mechanism through microchannels of monolith. Fig. 12. Langmuir-Hinshelwood model fitting with experimental data for photocatalytic CO 2 reduction with H 2 O using monolith photoreactor. Fig. 13.Comparison of the proposed kinetic model fitting-well with the empirical profile of CH 4 formation form photocatalytic CO 2 reduction with H 2 O using monolith photoreactor. Summary of physiochemical characteristics of TiO 2 nanoparticles and MMTTiO 2 nanocomposites. BET surface area BJH surface area External surface area Microporous surface area BJH pore volume Micropore volume BJH pore width a MMT 10 wt. in TiO 2 . Comparison between the cell type and microchannel monolith photoreactor using TiO 2 and MMTTiO 2 catalysts. Summary of product yield rates produced during photocatalytic CO 2 reduction using different catalysts and photoreactors.A CH 2 , A CH 3 and finally CH 4 and higher hydrocarbons.All possible reaction steps during photocatalytic CO 2 reduction with H 2 O are explained by a Yield rates calculated at 10 h irradiation basis, P H2 O 0.042 bar and P CO2 of 0.040 bar.ates . The value of CO 2 adsorption constant K CO 2 , was 30 bars A1 , that was 34.67-fold higher than that of H 2 O K H 2 O 14 0:75 bar A1 .The value of K H 2 O was less than one, indicating very weak adsorption of H 2 O while CO 2 was adsorbed very strongly over MMT modified TiO 2 surface.As discussed in the introduction part, a number of hydroxyl groups (OH) are present in MMT structure, and the addition of MMT into TiO 2 could be suitable for more CO 2 adsorption. Adsorption equilibrium and rate constants of Langmuir-Hinshelwood model estimated using experimental data on MMTTiO 2 catalyst. Summary of kinetic constants for fitting model with experimental data.decreasesby using MMTTiO 2 supported microchannel monolith due to strong mobility of intermediate products to produce the ultimate final products.
